sources.title: FAA Advisory Circular 150/5360-13A Airport Terminal Planning
FAA AC 150/5360-12F Airport Signing and Graphics

small.name: Typical Small Commercial Terminal
small.notes: Regional airport; 2-4 gates; single checkpoint; one baggage claim hall

medium.name: John Glenn Columbus International Terminal
medium.notes: Mid-size O&D terminal with ticketing, security, concourses, concessions, and claim

large.name: Moynihan Train Hall style passenger hall precedent not airport-specific
large.notes: Placeholder for large hall scale; replace with airport-specific precedent when calibrating

space_library.name: Check-In / Ticketing Hall
notes: FAA identifies passenger check-in/ticketing before security as a core terminal processor function; self-service kiosks can shift some processing to curbside or garage.

space_library.name: Security Screening Checkpoint
notes: FAA notes checkpoints are typically after the check-in/ticketing lobby and include offices/support for security staff, airport police, and emergency responders.

space_library.name: Concourse / Holdrooms
notes: FAA states holdrooms are where departing passengers wait and board flights; sizing depends on largest aircraft at the gate, passenger behavior, carry-on baggage, seating ratio, and processing objectives.

space_library.name: Passenger Boarding Gate / Gate Podium
notes: Gate podiums pair with holdrooms and passenger boarding bridges or ground-loaded operations.

space_library.name: Baggage Claim Hall
notes: FAA describes baggage claim as the reclaim area for arriving passengers and meeters/greeters; typical components include claim devices, circulation, seating, customer support, rental car/ground transportation concessions, restrooms, and mechanical support.

space_library.name: Baggage Service Office
notes: FAA identifies baggage service offices as airline spaces for lost baggage assistance and unclaimed bag storage.

space_library.name: Curbside / Landside Interface
notes: FAA lists curbside, pedestrian walkways, parking, access roadways, and transit links as primary landside terminal components.

space_library.name: Concessions / Passenger Amenities
notes: FAA identifies concessions and amenities in terminal processor, concourse, and baggage claim areas.

space_library.name: Public Restrooms
notes: FAA notes restrooms are support areas commonly grouped at ticketing, departures, and baggage claim.

space_library.name: Airport Ops / Police / Security Admin
notes: FAA notes security checkpoint areas typically include offices and support areas for administration, security staff, airport police, and emergency responders.

space_library.name: Baggage Claim / Output
notes: Outbound baggage processing area adjacent to check-in; includes conveyor systems, TSA screening interfaces, and baggage make-up devices.

space_sequences.name: Departing Passenger
space_sequences.description: Landside-to-airside progression: curbside → check-in/ticketing → security screening → concourse → gate → boarding. Core terminal processor sequence per FAA AC 150/5360-13A.

space_sequences.name: Arriving Passenger
space_sequences.description: Airside-to-landside: gate → concourse → baggage claim → curbside. Bypasses security in reverse direction. Post-security areas are sterile.

space_sequences.name: Outbound Baggage
space_sequences.description: Check-in counter → outbound baggage handling → aircraft. Runs parallel to departing passenger flow below the concourse level.

space_sequences.name: Inbound Baggage
space_sequences.description: Aircraft → baggage handling → claim devices → passenger pickup. Converges with arriving passenger at baggage claim hall.

space_sequences.name: Concessions / Amenities
space_sequences.description: Post-security retail and amenity loop off the concourse. Passengers browse concessions while moving toward gates. Not a mandatory sequence — overlays on departing passenger flow.

envelope_assembly.wall: high-performance curtain wall + insulated metal panel
envelope_assembly.glazing: low-e insulated glazing tuned to orientation

plan_archetypes.notes: Airside gate geometry should drive processor and landside curb planning.
